subject: One more thought
body:
Neal,

One thing I've noticed working with operations teams at manufacturing companies like yours-when equipment hits that 8-10 year mark, the support costs usually spike before they level off.

Not trying to push anything on you. But if you're looking at hardware spend projections for next year, TPM could cut that line item in half. And no more waiting for OEM support on legacy equipment.

Worth a conversation if you're in planning mode.

Brian

subject: Your peers are already doing this
body:
Neal,

I work with a lot of directors and managers at companies in the same space as Hexion. The ones who made the shift to TPM for their legacy equipment tell me the same thing-they wish they'd done it sooner.

The thing is, they didn't see it as a cost-saver at first. They just got tired of being on hold with OEM support.

If that sounds familiar, it's worth a 15-minute call.

Brian

subject: Last thought
body:
Neal,

I know you're probably juggling a lot right now. That's exactly why I wanted to reach out.

When you get to that point where legacy equipment becomes a problem-whether that's next quarter or next year-give me a call. We can have a quick conversation about what TPM looks like for your operation, no strings attached.

If it doesn't make sense for you, that's totally fine. But I'd rather you know it's an option.

Brian

subject: Quick check-in
body:
Neal,

Wanted to check in one more time. Have you had a chance to think about the hardware situation at Hexion?

No pressure at all if now isn't the right time. I just know that when you're ready to have that conversation, we can help.

Here's my cell if you want to reach out: 805.682.9358

Brian

subject: Thinking of you
body:
Neal,

I'm going to step back and let you be. But before I do, I wanted to make sure you have everything you need.

If the TPM option makes sense for Hexion at any point-whether that's now or down the line-you've got my number. Reach out whenever you're ready.

Hope things are going well.

Brian

What I hear pretty consistently from IT directors at distributed service companies is that maintenance is either already locked in or on the back burner until a contract renewal forces the conversation. Either one is fine.

If timing is off, just say the word and I'll leave you alone. If not, happy to find 15 minutes whenever it works.

subject: Nuclear plant network hardware. bulk rebuild idea
body:
Michael,

One thing I see a lot at utilities and nuclear sites is that whenever a major plant refresh happens, the network rebuild is typically last priority and always goes over budget.

It's because vendor budgets lock in first, and by the time the network team gets their hands on real hardware, you're usually 6-12 months away from power-on. so you build around whatever's available.

We handle it backwards. Work with your specs first, then source everything else from stock. Most of our utility and telecom customers save 20-30% on the full hardware package that way, and you get to specify what goes in your network rather than accept what the vendor has.

Might be worth an initial conversation. Happy to walk you through how it works.

Brian

It's one of those things that's hard to justify ripping out if it's still working.

We've built the whole company around supporting exactly that scenario. Might be a fit, might not be. but figured I'd ask.

subject: The transceiver sourcing problem nobody talks about
body:
James,

Most fiber operators I talk to buy transceivers one of two ways: direct from Cisco at full OEM price, or from a grey market supplier they're never quite sure about.

There's a third option. We manufacture and code transceivers for 40+ OEM platforms, 1G through 400G. Every unit is tested before it ships. No CLI workarounds, no compatibility guessing, fully coded for your gear. For a shop running a carrier neutral colo and fiber deployments across multiple MDU properties, the volume adds up fast.

If your current sourcing process has any friction, worth a quick conversation.

Brian

subject: Consolidating vendors-any interest?
body:
Jake-

Most of our customers start because they're tired of juggling three or four vendors just to keep their infrastructure current. By the time you've got RFQs out to Cisco, Juniper, Arista, and then your refurb supplier, you've lost weeks.

We handle it all. Routers, switches, optics, DIMMs, servers-one order, one invoice, one relationship. Harder for OEMs to do, easier for us to deliver.

Does your team ever talk about vendor consolidation?

Brian

subject: Why infrastructure directors push back on OEM pricing
body:
Hi Kal,

Most infrastructure leaders I talk to are in the same spot. they've got aging hardware across the branches, OEM renewals are coming due, and the cost doesn't make sense anymore.

We handle post-OEM support for that exact scenario. Servers, storage, optics. whatever you've got that's out of warranty, we can cover it at a fraction of OEM pricing and give you the flexibility to refresh on your timeline, not theirs.

No pressure if timing's not right. But if you're scoping budget for the next refresh cycle, it's worth knowing you have options.

Brian

With a network that size coming together, there's gear at every stage of the lifecycle. some of it probably coming off OEM support in the next year or two. The options are usually renew at full price, refresh early, or find a third party.

We run Systain, a Gartner-recognized third party maintenance program. Multi-vendor, 24/7 coverage, roughly half of what OEM charges. Teams use it to extend the life of hardware that still works fine but is past the OEM window.

If any contracts are coming up, worth a look.
